design_philosophy.core_loop: Select a giant monster, traverse side-scrolling levels fighting themed minions, culminate each level in grapple-heavy boss battles via button mashing, then face all bosses again in final stage before confronting the alien threat
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Monster vs. Monster dominance—players must prove their selected creature's superiority across global stages while managing the escalating alien invasion threat
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Command a giant mutated monster (dragon-like, cyborg, or atomic warrior) to defend Earth against alien invaders, with emphasis on physical dominance through grappling and raw power
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: The game doubles down on the first title's grapple-centric boss design philosophy; Genesis port radically reinterprets the formula as pure 1v1 fighting game rather than beat 'em up, suggesting SNK's original vision needed genre translation for home console audiences

difficulty.checkpointing: Level-based checkpoints; progression gate occurs at final stage where all defeated bosses must be fought again in succession before final boss encounter
difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: High agency in original arcade/Neo Geo version through co-op partnership (two players can join forces); ranking system (Good through Mystery Zone) tied to performance rewards suggests difficulty scaling through point thresholds rather than explicit selectable modes

progression.upgrade_system: Character selection at start determines moveset; no mid-game progression upgrades; Genesis port adds playable boss monsters as unlock rewards, expanding character roster
progression.unlock_mechanism: Genesis port unlocks boss monsters (all except final boss Famardy) as playable characters; original arcade/Neo Geo progression is linear with no unlocks
replayability_hooks: Three distinct playable monsters with different movesets (Super Geon, Cyber Woo, Atomic Guy)
Point-based ranking system (10 tiers) incentivizes high-score replays
Co-op vs single-player variation affects strategy
Genesis version adds boss monster playability as unlock content
Multiple endings: 'bad ending' if final remnants not destroyed within time limit; 'good ending' for successful destruction

world_building_techniques: Global stage progression (American City, French City, Grand Canyon, Desert, Sea Bed, Lava Zone, Enemy Hideout) establishes worldwide scope
Plot framing: three surviving monsters from 'King of the Monsters Massacre' must unite against alien invasion in 1999
Monster character narratives through design: Super Geon (mutated into dragon), Cyber Woo (cyborg refurbishment), Atomic Guy (powered-up variant)
Alien invasion escalation: minions first, then stage bosses, then final boss confrontation creates narrative throughline

boss_design.philosophy: Escalating alien threat hierarchy; each stage introduces thematically-named alien boss (frog, giant, two-headed, insectoid, aquatic, lava gremlin, final blob entity) representing environmental adaptation of invasion force. Boss design emphasizes grapple mechanics and button-mashing execution over pattern complexity
notable_bosses: Huge Keal/Frogger (Stage 1): Establishes grapple foundation with humanoid frog in helmet
Eifflelyte/Horn du Out (Stage 2): Giant with parasitic blob attachment—introduces multi-target mechanics
Clawhead/Yam a Mordon (Stage 3): Two-headed backward walker—unconventional movement pattern
Beetle Master/Kili-Kili (Stage 4): Giant insectoid with domed brain—visual mutation complexity
Aqua Slug/Sack Eyes (Stage 5): Aquatic snail in spiked shell—environmental hazard integration
Lavicus/Eat Wow (Stage 6): Gremlin-like creature spawning minion eggs—adds add-on threat mechanic

Famardy (Final Boss): Massive blob monster with multiple eyes and fetus-like tongue—ultimate alien form, player cannot play as this character even in Genesis port, emphasizing its unique threat status

faction_indicators: Monster allegiance: Human-allied survivors (Super Geon, Cyber Woo, Atomic Guy) vs. Alien invaders (all named boss entities)
Planetary defense coalition implied through global stage progression and end-game facing of all bosses
Genesis port expands play-as-boss mechanics, potentially suggesting assimilation/conversion themes in character unlock design
danger_signals: Alien invasion threat explicitly framed in plot ('1999' temporal marker suggests fin-de-siècle apocalypse anxiety)
Escalating boss difficulty and minion waves indicate invasion intensity
Final stage forcing re-engagement with all previously defeated bosses signals desperation/desperation defense
Time-limited destruction of final boss remnants creates urgency and 'bad ending' penalty, signaling incomplete victory
environmental_storytelling: Stage progression across Earth locations (American City → French City → Grand Canyon → Desert → Sea Bed → Lava Zone → Enemy Hideout) maps invasion geography
Boss environmental adaptation (aquatic slug in sea bed, lava creature in lava zone) suggests alien ecology/colonization
Minion monster types tied to stage environments (Sand Worm in desert, Shark Fin/Blade Ray in sea, Dragon Skull as air threat)
Genesis port adds Japanese cities (Kyoto, Tokyo, Osaka) as invasion zones, expanding global stakes
